frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
generated_comment: Honestly, I don't see what the fuss is about. Colleges have every right, even a responsibility, to maintain discipline and decorum. This dress code isn't about control; it's about fostering an environment of respect and seriousness for our young women. When girls dress appropriately, it reflects well on them and our institutions, upholding the sanskar we try to instill. Without such standards, we risk eroding our cultural values and inviting unnecessary distractions. The college is simply doing its part to ensure a dignified learning space.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment frames regulating women's clothing as necessary for 'discipline,' 'decorum,' and 'respect,' implying that 'modern' attire ('without such standards') leads to 'eroding cultural values' and 'unnecessary distractions.' It subtly blames women's dress choices for societal issues and justifies controlling their appearance under the guise of 'dignity' and 'safeguarding traditions,' which is a form of benevolent sexism that restricts women's autonomy.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
